snippets.title: The fundamental loop
snippets.code:
def summarize(text: str) -> str:
    chunks = chunk(text)
    breakpoint()                  # land here, inspect chunks
    payload = build_prompt(chunks)
    breakpoint()                  # inspect the payload before sending
    return call_llm(payload)


summarize(open('doc.txt').read())

# In the terminal:
# (Pdb) p len(chunks)
# 12
# (Pdb) pp chunks[:2]
# ['First chunk text...', 'Second chunk text...']
# (Pdb) c            # continue to next breakpoint
snippets.explanation: Two breakpoints; inspect the state at each; `c` to move on. No prints, no reruns.

snippets.title: Cheat-sheet — the commands you'll use 95% of the time
snippets.code:
(Pdb) help                # list of commands
(Pdb) n                   # next line (same frame)
(Pdb) s                   # step INTO a function call
(Pdb) r                   # run until current function returns
(Pdb) c                   # continue to next breakpoint / end
(Pdb) l                   # show 11 lines around current
(Pdb) ll                  # long-list: entire current function
(Pdb) p expr              # print value of expr
(Pdb) pp expr             # pretty-print
(Pdb) w                   # stack trace (where am I?)
(Pdb) u                   # move UP a frame
(Pdb) d                   # move DOWN a frame
(Pdb) b file.py:42        # set breakpoint at line 42
(Pdb) b chunker:42, len(text) > 4000   # conditional breakpoint
(Pdb) cl                  # clear ALL breakpoints
(Pdb) q                   # quit
# At the prompt you can also evaluate ANY Python expression:
(Pdb) [x.id for x in docs if x.score > 0.8]
snippets.explanation: If you remember n / s / c / p / w / u / d you've got 95% of pdb. Everything else is occasional sugar.

snippets.title: Post-mortem on the last exception
snippets.code:
# Launch script under pdb; it auto-enters post-mortem on uncaught exceptions:
python -m pdb -c continue script.py

# Or programmatically:
# import sys, pdb
# try:
#     main()
# except Exception:
#     pdb.post_mortem(sys.exc_info()[2])

# In Jupyter:
# %pdb on      # auto-drop into pdb on any exception
# %debug      # drop into pdb on the LAST exception
snippets.explanation: `python -m pdb -c continue` runs normally, then drops you into pdb at the moment of the crash. Beats reproducing the bug.

snippets.title: Make breakpoint() use ipdb everywhere
snippets.code:
pip install ipdb

# Use ipdb for all `breakpoint()` calls:
export PYTHONBREAKPOINT=ipdb.set_trace
python script.py

# Or for one-off:
PYTHONBREAKPOINT=ipdb.set_trace python script.py

# Disable all breakpoints (useful in production):
PYTHONBREAKPOINT=0 python script.py
snippets.explanation: `PYTHONBREAKPOINT` is a one-line, zero-code-change way to switch debugger flavour — or kill all breakpoints.

snippets.title: Pretty-printing big objects
snippets.code:
(Pdb) import json
(Pdb) print(json.dumps(payload, indent=2)[:500])
(Pdb) pp messages           # pdb's built-in pretty-printer
(Pdb) import pprint
(Pdb) pprint.pprint(messages, depth=2, width=120)
(Pdb) !import dataclasses; dataclasses.asdict(config)   # `!` runs a statement
snippets.explanation: Use `pp` for default pretty-printing; for finer control, you have the full Python stdlib at the prompt. `!` runs a statement (vs evaluating an expression).
